author: @yohell
text: The important part to convey of the dangers of electricity obviously isn't "don't make mistakes or you'll die", because that isn't very helpful, and can be said about anything from cars to plutonium. We are human and will make mistakes. The more useful video would perhaps be one that illustrate ways to reduce the probability of making mistakes, and the consequences of making mistakes. Like the one you made where you added protective measures to the fractal wood burner, adding safety features to a terribly dangerous machine. Now, people have become more aware that this type of machine is terribly dangerous, and that making them and using them probably isn't a healthy pastime, which is why I feel your explaining the safety features and reasoning about the risks and mitigations you implement for that particular machine are particularly effective. I think that video was a little bit scary, but very effective, and very much hitting the mark of safety - the identification and reasoning around risks and mitigations. From this video however, I got more of a "don't make mistakes or you'll die" vibe, which approach I think will not do much to sway people who are generally worried about the dangers of electricity.

author: @TheRebelmanone
text:
Since you are talking safety i want to bring to attention the wire sizes. Like you were saying the wire in essence is a conductor as well as a resistor.  But you did later say it, you said if you exceed the limit of the wire it burns. Same thing happens to any resistor, if you exceed the limit it burns, this is unlike water. 
If you hook for example a 12 gauge wire  to your mains and then hook a 0 gauge wire to the 12 gauge as the "resistor"(like the light bulb, but in this case it is a huge thick wire), then the "resistor" has much higher limit than the wire supplying it, therefore the wire supplying it burns. This is where the similarities of electricity and water differ, the "pipe"(wire) will melt and burn your house down if it is too small for the pressure(voltage) and flow(current) you are forcing thru it. But fortunately the supply wire is always hooked to the right sized circuit breaker, and once it goes to exceed the limit of the wire the breaker shuts it all off.. That is your real safety, circuit breakers and grounds, and knowing how to size wire with the right breakers. Ground fault breakers too.

The safety is not really needed in the device itself, but still most have a fuse, or a breaker built in the device, especially high energy devices. But the safety is in the RIGHT size wiring supplying it, the RIGHT size breaker in your breaker box in the house, and ground fault breakers installed. That grounding stake is the most important ground in your entire house, but they are all important. That gnd. stake is for if anything shorts to the casing, instead of it sitting there live waiting for you to touch it and get killed, it already tripped the circuit breaker because the energy flows thru the gnd to that gnd rod outside near your service entrance panel, and causes high current flow tripping the breaker instantly, protecting you, now if you touch the case where the device shorted, it can't electrocute you, the breaker shut it off. Even if it shorted as you were touching it, you still would not get electrocuted because electricity takes path of least resistance and will rather flow effortlessly to the ground thru the rod, and still trip the breaker as well, even if you were touching it the entire time. But if you were grounded BETTER than the grounding rod, then you will get electrocuted anyway, but that is almost impossible to become more conductive than copper and iron rods. At most you will feel a tingle, because the bulk of the energy went to gnd thru the rod, only a small amount went thru you, not even harming you at all. BUT if your gnd rod is faulty or corroded away, then you will get electrocuted if a short happens to any case of any device or appliance with conductive case. Even the electrical boxes installed in houses that are steel will be grounded to the rod too, to prevent any shorts in the boxes from electrocuting you also.

author: @RWBHere
text:
Another vital requirement, even for a highly skilled electrician, is: Don't become blasé about it. It's all too easy to do the same thing a thousand times, and to forget about the dangers. That's when you get hurt. Most electricians get a few electric shocks before they learn the lesson.

A real example: I once replaced a socket on a 240 Volt extension lead, having put the mains plug into my pocket for safety; the simplest way to do it. I forgot to check that the wall socket switch was turned off; something which you might think was superfluous. But, after finishing the job, I went to put the plug back into the socket, only to find that _the wrong plug was in my pocket, and that the power was still turned on._ To this day, I don't know how I didn't receive an electric shock from those wires which I had stripped by using side cutters and twisted into shape with bare hands. Lesson learned.

author: @SwitchingNoise
text: At 50 and 60 Hz, the skin effect is irrelevant. The kettle wiring is stranded because otherwise it wouldn't be very flexible (solid wires would just break if the kettle would be moved a few times). Stranded wires for high frequencies, also called Litz wire, are individually insulated strands otherwise it would still be a solid conductor. Litz wire is being used for transformers in switch mode power supplies, RF transformers,... An other variant is the hollow conductors. They are being used in high power RF circuits like radars for example. Your explanation about impedance is also not correct: impedance is being used in circuits with reactive components (inductors, capacitors). For circuit without reactive components, you use resistance. Also, the heater you've got is made from aluminium oxide or ceramic.

There will be TV engineers watching this, shaking their heads and going ... "Wait until you get zapped by 20kv off the top cap of the HT valve in a TV!" LOL

One reason why experienced engineers try their best to only use one hand - the right hand when probing around live mains powered devices and equipment. It's then a shock to ground down your right side and hopefully through high resistance shoe soles (rubber) so it doesn't actually go across your heart! 

OBVIOUSLY, leaning on the grounded metal chassis or enclosure with bare hands is NOT recommended either! 🤔😎

author: @kolinevans9127

One of the most interesting exciting and {as far as I know } successful safety feature that might make people laugh is to look at a Microwave Oven Schematic, the Plate of the magnetron, {which is a vacuum tube} is connected directly to the frame of the microwave.
This means that the 2000+ V {reasonable high current} connection is connected directly to the frame of the microwave oven
Then that frame of the oven is connected to earth ground via the input cable. 
I did a double take and laughed when I saw that first, however it logically makes sense in some ingenious way, as it solves a number of potential {no pun} problems.

author: @will_doherty
text: Motors are a special case - if you put their rated voltage across the armature that you had - or into a seized or locked motor - without adequate protection, it will as the technical expression is, go bang. Motors rely on the back emf (the electricity they produce as a generator whilst spinning, which opposes the applied voltage) to limit the current they draw. This has the great advantage of high starting torque reducing to a lesser value (alongside the current) when up to speed, without the need for additional controls.

author: @ciaransherry6021
text:
Hi Rob. Could you please tell folks to always make sure that any circuit into which they connect, should always have an upstream current leakage device of no more than 30mA.
In the event of an inadvertent hazardous human connection with the circuit to which they have connected occurring, the device will interrupt current flow in less than 50ms.
Many thanks, and I love your channel, wouldn't like to see anything happen to you or my fellow followers.
Ciaran.

author: @buccleuch7621
text:
Never fails to surprise me the number of consumer electrical devices that are supplied with the wrong rated fuse (usually 13 amp) when a 3 or 5 amp will suffice,  be much safer and more protective of the device, as well as the device user.
First thing I do with any new equipment is check the fuse rating and usually put in a lower rated fuse. Particularly surprises me that despite improved electrical efficiency, a standard size 1, 1.5 or 2 amp fuse is not commonly available here in the UK. Is it because the manufacturers want consumers to blow your machines or they don't understand electrics?
Keep up the great work!

author: @BurnabyAlex
text:
If you had experience with the wiring industry, and electronics industry,  you will find that a lot of standards are due to previous failures .
1 - Not all rubber hoses insulate - you may have an insulating rubber hose, or you may have a rubber hose with carbon black added which conducts,  I've seen molded silicone boots added for safety over electrical contacts actually catch fire and require a recall.
2. Different insulators will have different temperature ratings.  If you're making an oven, use oven wire.  
Every wire you buy will have a data sheet for the current the conductor can carry, and the volts it can insulate for, as well as whether it is rated for outdoor use.  Some coatings fail to work when wet, hence indoor only extension cords.
3. Metals melt when you have overcurrent.  I've seen solder joints at the input liquefy, and flow to the next solder joint creating a local arc furnace at 24 volts, 40 amps.    Your copper pipe holders can melt as well, and the insulation between the 2 conductors in the wire can melt  causing an arc where you don't expect it.  
4. Like other people mentioned, different solutions create different gases.  Some harmless, some to watch out for.
Best of luck, and always check the data sheets of the products you use to verify it can handle voltage, current, moisture.
Welding cables are cheap, and oven wire can be found for cheap or from old ovens.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers for the post mate - thanks for taking the time to write

replies.author: @BurnabyAlex
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Hope you do well!

author: @universebecomingltd
text:
When I was 16 years old or so, I took apart a simple camera.  It was just a simple battery-powered camera with a built-in flash unit.  Well, while I was messing with disassembling it for around 15 minutes I thought, hey, I wonder if that big electrolytic capacitor has a charge in it?  HAHA!  🤣🤣  So I stuck the tip of a screwdriver across the terminals and POW!!  It was fully charged!  The discharge completely vaporized the tip of the screwdriver in a blinding white flash!  It was rated at something like 300 microfarads at 320 volts!  Not enough joules to kill but that was a real attention-getter let me tell ya!  That scared me so bad that I from then on had an extreme respect for electricity!  😁

Well, not extreme enough though.  It took another event to actually convince me to be more cautious.  Also around that time, I had built a simple high voltage device out of an ignition coil from an automobile.  It'd make somewhat friendly-looking 2-centimeter blueish white sparks at 60 Hz.  You could touch the sparks and they'd sting quite a bit, but no big deal really.  No big deal until I grabbed ahold of the two live high voltage terminals with both hands.  NOW THAT WAS DIFFERENT!  😁  The muscles in my fingers locked onto the terminals and I couldn't let go!  My whole body was STRONGLY buzzing at the frequency of the device and I started to die as every muscle in my body was immobilized.  As I started to fall unconscious my body slowly fell to the left as I slowly fell out of the chair and the weight of my body pulled my hands free and I was fine after a few minutes of hyperventilating.  SO THAT FINALLY CONVINCED ME TO BE EXTRA EXTRA CAREFUL!  HAHAHA!  🤣🤣🤣

It's forty years later and I haven't had another close call.

replies.author: @derekp2674
replies.text: Given that Robert's videos often feature the dismantling of old equipment and the re-use of salvaged components, it was disappointing to see no mention of safety precautions for handling and storing capacitors in the video.  Where I used to work, we would always check that capacitors had been short circuited and earthed down before handling them and we would store all large capacitors with an earthing wire across their terminals, to prevent them becoming recharged by voltage hysterysis.

author: @williamarmstrong7199
text:
I learn something new on every video! I knew electricity travels on the outside of the conductor, but I did not know the frequency dictates how deep into the wire the flow happens. 
That is very interesting. I wonder why that happens.

replies.author: @worxharder9470
replies.text:
I think of it like this, imagine a log on fire. The wire is the log. The more frequency there is, the deeper it will go. With low frequency (low fire) the log would barely be effected.

Electrons prefer to travel along the surface, but at high enough frequency, they will actually start breaking down into the metal of the conductor, because there are only so many along the surface at any given time. 
Now imagine a wire exposed to ultra high frequency. I wonder if conductors have limitations on frequency once every electron in a wire is utilized, even at it's core. Would it pull electrons from surrounding air?
At least that's how I imagine it.

replies.author: @derekp2674
replies.text:
If you google "ac skin effect" or something like that you should learn more...

In brief summary, the skin effect is a feature of the laws of electromagnetic induction, as used in domestic induction hobs. If a thick wire is connected to a power supply, a current will begin to flow along the wire. In turn the current will establish a magnetic field around the wire. If the wire is surround by free space, e.g. air or vacuum, the magnetic field can appear more or less instantly. So, if the current rise from zero to maximum in a short time, e.g. 5ms, the the magnetic field strength outside the wire will also reach its maximum in that time. If the current is confined to the outside of the wire, then there will not be any magnetic field inside the wire. But, if current is also going to flow inside the body of the wire, then there must also be a magnetic field there. Unfortunately, whenever a current acts so as to cause a time changing magnetic field in a conductor, that field then acts so as to produce opposing currents and voltage in that conductor. So for any ac conductor, there is a battle between the electric fields (or "voltages") produced by any resistance along its skin, which tends to drive the current to flow down into the body of the wire and the voltages produced by electromagnetic induction, which counteract the tendency for the current to flow over more than just the surface of the wire.

With the aid of properly detailed physics and maths you can calculate that the effective skin depth in a copper wire will be (66mm / the square root of the ac frequency in cycles per second or Hertz).

replies.author: @derekp2674
replies.text: @worxharder9470 Er no - the electrical skin depth actually decreases as the reciprocal of the square root of the frequency.

replies.author: @worxharder9470
replies.text: @derekp2674 thanks for the info, I have always understood magnetic fields to be in the shape of a toroid. And have always imagined that shape to run along the length of a conductor. So I could see your info being accurate, seeing how a toroid has a hollow center. I'm no physicist but still I try to grasp things in laymen's terms, just to further my understanding. I will look further in that skin affect. I had no clue there was info out there on it, thanks again.

author: @MerwinARTist
text: Water (a form of energy) flowing in a pipe is created with a pump with a pipe in a pond.  Electricity is a frequency flowing in a pipe of insulated wire (antenna) .. by a generator.  What is the pond?

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text: Would that be the battery or other energy storage device? A lake stores water, a battery stores energy, but so does a fly wheel or a weight at the top of a tower or a spring...  A battery is probably more like a lake of electricity: tap it and it flows. The latter 3 three can turn an electric generator, also causing the flow of electrons, but they could also power a water pump. So those wouldn't be "lakes" for electricity, but the potential energy storage would be the "lake" for mechanical energy.

replies.author: @BirdmanNorfolk
replies.text: It's been a few years since my electrical theory, I am 54, but as far as I remember the difference is the PSSC, that is the assumed or tested permissive short circuit current on a conductor, whilst live and neutral phases have the potential for a serious shock, the killer literally is to complete the live circuit to Earth with your body, hence as technicians we were always taught to keep one hand in our back trouser pocket when working on a live circuit, particularly while carrying out test and inspection of anything unknown, people do sometimes tinker with appliances, so when you are fault finding you always treat them as potentially dangerous, the other thing we did of course was unplug the kit and keep the plug in sight while working, I have never received a shock from a live appliance, though I did once get one from an isolated circuit, because the capacitors hadn't fully discharged!😬

author: @clarencementor4862
text: Hi Robert have you ever wondered why companies do not make electric heaters from graphite material. Graphite, Graphene and carbon have a distinct property when used as a heating element. It behaves as a supper-conductor as it heats up. Other than metals it resistance goes down when heated up. Metals on the other hand increases in resistance as it heats up. To sum it all up more efficient heating elements can be made from graphite.

replies.author: @derekp2674
replies.text: To quickly answer your query about electrons, a flow of current can involve the motion of electrons but does not have to. There is also "displacement current" which occurs where you have a changing electric field, such as a changing voltage across the insulation in a capacitor. James Clerk Maxwell set out the physical laws that govern this in what is now know as the Maxwell-Ampere law.

author: @RagingGuppy
text: I remember when taking electronics in school. Its not the voltage that kills you its the current. 1 amp of current is enough to kill you.

replies.author: @AlienRelics
replies.text: 100mA of current is enough to kill you. A large percentage of people can't let go with 20mA or more. If your skin is pierced in the right (wrong) places, 12V can kill you, and 5V might even kill you.

replies.author: @barry2718
replies.text:
Skin is Not an issue until the frequency exceeds a 1MHz usually. The reason main power wires are twisted is flexibility not only in use but for transport when coiled on a drum. Basic fact is bending a 7 or 19 strand wire is easier than the same cross sectional area single strand. 
At 10kV and over the maximum outside diameter has to be increase to reduce the corona effect in air as air is ionized with the electric field intensity. Sharp edges have to be avoided. That is a completely different thing to skin effect.

replies.author: @barry2718
replies.text: Great video covered the golden rules of safety. Another one I'd to only use one hand on the job. The body impedance at 50 hz is low ( mainly capacitance ) and with one hand on active there is only a tiny ac current to earth via the bodies capacitance assuming your insulated from earth that is. Still very dangerous.

author: @ThisRandomUsername
text:
Hi Rob. Just a quick mention which I'm sure you've simplified for the video: At 50/60Hz, the skin effect in a conductor is around 8mm, so typically only the HV transmission lines are stranded for this reason. The reason you have stranded wire in household appliances is just for flexibility and durability. Same with wiring in your walls, typically the lower current circuits in your house are wired with a single conductor, as it's thinner than the same current-carrying capacity stranded wire.

At much higher frequencies, the skin effect is a lot thinner, so they have insulated strands of wire twisted together. Look up litz wire. A couple years ago I made some 125kHz RFID coils out of it (carrying something like 50mA). It did improve things a little, but it was more effort than it was worth.

author: @michaelinamoment
text: Great explanation. I use a 9.5 watt bulb whenever I'm doing something new in order to make sure I don't accidentally shock myself with mains AC at full blast. on occasion I have shocked myself with that 9.5 watts and it definitely lets you know. Sort of like a strong electric  cattle fence.

replies.author: @will_doherty
replies.text: 9.5 W @ 240V is very close to a current of 40 milliamps. 10 milliamps across the heart is considered to be sufficient to kill some people, 40milliamps across the heart will likely kill many. Since you initially offer a much higher resistance than the lamp, you'll get the full 240V to drive it through you too. I wouldn't rely on your lamp too much to keep you alive.

replies.author: @derekp2674
replies.text: @will_doherty Thanks for mentioning this. I think it was negligent that Robert did not mention this in the video.

@colleenforrest7936 great advice thank you.

People need to also consider their usual environments - spending a day shopping, those who work in retail (always around high levels of radio frequency activity if footfall is busy) children's schools, hospitals. But also older people in care homes, those who simply stay in all day (near WiFi router, mobile phone, smart devices).

The EU document linked above states the recent science indicates a "high hazard risk to ecosystems and species". 

It also urges member nations to hold Moratoriums - inform their populations for debate ... sadly the government, the BBC,  ITV and rest it seems, have yet failed to do so. 

Any RF or technical experts out there, please look into it for your loved ones. And for mine 👍

Your first taste of high voltage was similar to mine, on a spark plug testing machine early one morning, it's amazing how 30,000 Volts soon wakes you up!
